title: Contextual Subspace Manifold Projection for Structural Refinement of Large Language Model Representations
authors: Alistair Wren, Beatrice Loxley, Hamish Cadwallader, Simon Beckwith, Fabian Pargeter, James Blades
abstract: Internal representations within deep neural architectures encode high-dimensional abstractions of linguistic structures, yet they often exhibit inefficiencies in feature distribution, limiting expressiveness and adaptability. Contextual Subspace Manifold Projection introduces a structured refinement technique that selectively reconfigures token embeddings through controlled subspace constraints, ensuring more stable and geometrically well-defined feature distributions. Empirical evaluations demonstrated that the structured intervention reduced anisotropy, leading to improved representation compactness while preserving semantic fidelity across transformer layers. Clustering analyses indicated that token embeddings exhibited greater feature separability, reinforcing the hypothesis that structured projection techniques enhance internal representation organization without sacrificing linguistic coherence. Gradient magnitude distributions suggested that the method introduced a smoother optimization trajectory, potentially contributing to more stable parameter updates throughout training. Computational overhead associated with the projection operations remained minimal, ensuring that the refinements did not introduce significant trade-offs in model efficiency or inference speed. Comparisons with standard embedding refinement techniques highlighted that structured manifold constraints provided a direct mechanism for improving representation quality without requiring additional gradient-based optimization. Perplexity evaluations confirmed that the adjustments did not negatively impact sequence coherence, further validating the effectiveness of the proposed approach.

paragraphs: Language models have undergone extensive advancements in recent years, leading to increasingly sophisticated architectures capable of handling complex reasoning tasks, contextual dependencies, and multimodal integration. The underlying representations within these models encode high-dimensional abstractions of linguistic structures, semantic relationships, and syntactic patterns. Despite their widespread deployment across applications ranging from automated writing assistants to scientific discovery tools, an incomplete understanding remains regarding how internal representations evolve and interact throughout the layers of deep transformer networks. The representations embedded within neural activations determine model generalization, response coherence, and adaptability to diverse prompts, yet the mechanisms governing representational refinement remain largely unexplored. Identifying techniques that enable structured adjustments to these internal activations presents an opportunity to improve the expressiveness, efficiency, and stability of language models without requiring additional external training corpora or supervised fine-tuning.

A significant challenge in refining latent representations within LLMs lies in the inherently complex geometry of the learned feature spaces. Representations occupy high-dimensional manifolds shaped by self-attention mechanisms and non-linear transformations across multiple layers. Conventional approaches to model refinement primarily focus on loss function adjustments, hyperparameter tuning, or additional pretraining, yet these methods fail to provide direct control over the structure of internal representations. Without explicit constraints on how embedding distributions evolve across layers, models often develop redundancies, overparameterization, or anisotropic representations that hinder generalization. The absence of systematic methods for controlling representational structures limits interpretability, creates inefficiencies in downstream applications, and complicates transfer learning across diverse domains.

A novel methodology, Contextual Subspace Manifold Projection (CSMP), is introduced to refine the internal representations of LLMs via structured adjustments within learned embedding spaces. Unlike traditional fine-tuning, which relies on gradient updates to modify weight distributions, the proposed approach applies structured projection operations to latent feature spaces, ensuring that learned representations adhere to well-defined geometric constraints. CSMP enforces a subspace transformation within the contextual embeddings of the model, restructuring token activations while preserving information necessary for contextual coherence. Through controlled manifold adaptation, representational expressiveness can be improved, reducing overfitting while enhancing structural alignment across layers. Unlike conventional dimensionality reduction techniques, which impose global transformations across embeddings, the proposed method selectively restructures specific subspaces to facilitate more efficient representation utilization.

Experimental validation of CSMP involves integrating the method into a recent open-source LLM to assess its impact on internal feature distributions, token embeddings, and sequence-level coherence. A comprehensive evaluation is performed across multiple linguistic benchmarks to measure improvements in representation compactness, contextual stability, and semantic separability. The methodology operates without requiring additional external annotations, human supervision, or dataset expansion, making it computationally feasible for large-scale language models. The effectiveness of CSMP is assessed through quantitative metrics capturing representational geometry and qualitative analysis of model outputs, ensuring a rigorous investigation into its potential benefits.

Contributions of this work are summarized as follows. First, a novel subspace-based refinement technique is introduced for internal representation restructuring within LLMs. Second, an implementation of CSMP is developed within an existing open-source model, enabling empirical validation of its effectiveness. Third, a detailed analysis of the representational changes induced through manifold projection is presented, demonstrating the method’s capability to refine feature distributions while maintaining linguistic coherence. Finally, the broader implications of subspace-based control mechanisms for neural representation learning are explored, highlighting potential applications in model interpretability, efficiency optimization, and domain adaptation.

Advancements in large language models have been closely associated with improvements in internal representation learning, geometric structuring of neural activations, and subspace-based refinements within high-dimensional embedding spaces. Various studies have explored how feature distributions evolve across model layers, how contextual embeddings preserve syntactic and semantic relationships, and how constraints on representation geometry influence downstream performance [1]. However, limited research has explicitly investigated structured manifold projection techniques aimed at refining LLM representations through systematic subspace transformations [2]. The following subsections examine prior efforts related to internal representation analysis, geometric methods for neural embeddings, and the broader challenges associated with modifying learned feature structures in a controlled manner [3, 4].

subsections.title: II-A Internal Representation Analysis in Large Language Models
paragraphs: The internal activations of LLMs encode hierarchical linguistic abstractions through deep transformer layers, where contextual embeddings form complex distributions that evolve across multiple attention blocks [5]. Studies examining activation patterns within self-attention mechanisms have indicated that deeper layers tend to develop increasingly specialized representations, capturing high-level semantic dependencies while gradually discarding low-level token-specific details [6]. Layer-wise analysis of hidden states has shown that earlier layers primarily encode local lexical structures, whereas deeper layers refine broader contextual relationships across longer sequences [7]. Experiments involving representational similarity metrics have demonstrated that LLM embeddings exhibit anisotropic distributions, often concentrating activations within a restricted subspace of the overall high-dimensional space, leading to inefficiencies in representation utilization [8]. Several techniques, including redundancy suppression and attention modulation, have been explored to counteract excessive anisotropy and promote more uniform feature distributions [9, 10]. However, direct interventions in the structural properties of embeddings through subspace constraints remain underexplored in existing approaches [11].

subsections.title: II-B Geometric Structures in Neural Representations
paragraphs: The study of geometric structures within neural networks has provided insights into how learned feature spaces influence generalization, stability, and adaptability across various domains [12, 13]. Prior work has analyzed how self-attention mechanisms reshape token embeddings, revealing that attention heads often construct distinct subspaces that emphasize different syntactic or semantic relationships [14]. Investigations into representational clustering within transformer-based models have highlighted that token embeddings naturally align along low-dimensional manifolds, indicating inherent structural constraints imposed through sequential processing and contextual dependencies [15]. Certain methods have attempted to regularize embedding geometries via implicit constraints on curvature, orthogonality, and compactness, yet existing approaches primarily rely on indirect optimization objectives rather than explicit subspace projection techniques [16]. Subspace alignment methods have been explored in transfer learning settings to facilitate domain adaptation, but their application to internal representation refinement within LLMs remains largely absent from prior research [17].

subsections.title: II-C Contextual Embedding Refinements
paragraphs: Contextual embedding refinement strategies have been proposed to enhance language model generalization through targeted modifications of token representations at various processing stages [18]. Techniques such as contrastive representation learning, embedding normalization, and residual reweighting have been employed to improve semantic consistency while mitigating overfitting effects [19, 20]. Context-dependent reparameterization has been investigated as a means of introducing adaptive transformations within learned representations, yet such methods predominantly focus on input-level adjustments rather than structural modifications to internal activations [21]. Methods leveraging adversarial robustness have shown that perturbation-based refinements can enhance feature stability against minor input variations, yet these approaches often fail to introduce controlled modifications to the geometric organization of latent spaces [22]. Subspace-based interventions that systematically adjust token representations via structured projection techniques have yet to be extensively explored, limiting the ability to directly impose constraints on representation evolution within LLMs [23].

subsections.title: II-D Limitations of Current Representation Refinement Techniques
paragraphs: Despite the growing interest in improving LLM representation learning, existing refinement techniques often rely on indirect optimization objectives that fail to provide explicit control over feature transformations [24]. Traditional fine-tuning approaches adjust model weights based on task-specific objectives, yet such modifications lack direct influence over the structural properties of learned embeddings [25]. Representation regularization methods, such as weight decay and dropout, have been used to prevent overfitting, but they do not fundamentally reshape feature distributions in a controlled manner [26]. Manifold learning approaches have been applied to neural networks in other contexts, yet they remain largely absent from practical applications involving LLMs due to computational constraints and the complexity of high-dimensional representation spaces [27]. A systematic methodology that enforces structured modifications to token representations through subspace projection techniques could provide a viable solution to many of the challenges currently limiting representation refinement efforts in language models [28].

The representation space of a large language model encodes complex relationships between tokens, phrases, and contextual dependencies, yet existing architectures lack direct mechanisms to enforce structured modifications within these internal embeddings. Traditional fine-tuning approaches adjust model weights based on task-specific supervision, yet they provide limited control over the geometric organization of latent spaces. Contextual Subspace Manifold Projection (CSMP) introduces a structured intervention in representation learning through controlled transformations that refine token embeddings within predefined subspaces. The approach reconfigures internal activations without requiring additional gradient-based updates to model parameters, facilitating a direct mechanism to impose constraints on feature distributions while maintaining contextual coherence.

subsections.title: III-A Conceptual Framework
paragraphs: Contextual embeddings within LLMs evolve across multiple self-attention layers, where token representations undergo iterative transformations influenced through surrounding linguistic structures. The learned embedding space exhibits anisotropic tendencies, where representations concentrate within restricted regions of the high-dimensional manifold, limiting expressiveness and potentially reducing generalization capabilities. CSMP introduces an explicit subspace refinement technique that systematically projects token activations onto a structured manifold, ensuring that learned representations adhere to predefined geometric constraints while preserving their semantic fidelity.

The methodology operates through an orthogonality-preserving transformation that reshapes token embeddings while maintaining their contextual dependencies. Token representations are projected into a lower-dimensional structured subspace that enforces compactness while preventing excessive overlap between feature clusters. Through structured refinement, embeddings acquire improved separation between distinct linguistic concepts while retaining the inherent contextual information necessary for coherent text generation. Unlike dimensionality reduction methods that indiscriminately compress feature representations, the proposed approach selectively restructures feature activations within an optimally defined subspace, facilitating improved representation alignment across layers.

subsections.title: III-B Mathematical Formulation
paragraphs: Let X∈ℝd×nX\in\mathbb{R}^{d\times n} represent the token embeddings of an input sequence, where dd denotes the dimensionality of the embedding space and nn corresponds to the number of tokens. Standard self-attention mechanisms transform XX through learned weight matrices, producing a sequence of activations HlH_{l} at layer ll. Without explicit constraints, representations may diverge into suboptimal anisotropic distributions, leading to inefficient utilization of the representational space.

CSMP imposes a structured projection operation that maps the activations onto a constrained manifold, ensuring controlled evolution of token representations. Given an orthonormal basis U∈ℝd×kU\in\mathbb{R}^{d\times k} defining the target subspace, token embeddings are projected via the transformation:

where U​UTUU^{T} defines a rank-preserving projection matrix that enforces alignment with the target manifold. The projection operation maintains orthogonality between feature components, preserving essential information while suppressing redundancies.
The structured refinement further incorporates a stability-preserving regularization term that penalizes deviations from the projected subspace:

where the first term ensures projection consistency, and the second term enforces stability across transformer layers through a weighting factor λ\lambda. The overall objective refines token embeddings without requiring additional gradient updates to model parameters, facilitating an efficient and scalable approach to structured representation learning.

subsections.title: III-C Implementation in a Large Language Model
paragraphs: CSMP was integrated into an open-source transformer-based LLM through modifications to the embedding initialization and self-attention mechanisms. The structured projection was applied within the hidden state activations of intermediate transformer layers, ensuring that representation refinement occurred without interfering with standard self-attention operations. Token embeddings were preconditioned via an initial projection step that mapped input representations onto an optimized subspace, providing a structured initialization that improved feature separability.

Subsequent layers incorporated layer-wise manifold projections, maintaining consistency in representation alignment while allowing for controlled evolution of embeddings. The self-attention computation remained unchanged, ensuring that information propagation within the model preserved the original sequence dependencies. The projection matrices were dynamically adjusted based on the learned geometric structure of activations, facilitating an adaptive refinement strategy that maintained stability across inference steps.

subsections.title: III-D Computational Considerations
paragraphs: The structured projection approach introduced a minor computational overhead due to the additional matrix multiplications required to enforce subspace constraints. However, the operations were designed to be highly parallelizable, allowing efficient execution through batched tensor operations within modern deep learning frameworks. The primary computational cost arose from the evaluation of the orthonormal basis transformations, yet this was mitigated through precomputed projections that reduced per-inference latency.

Efficiency trade-offs were analyzed through empirical evaluations of training time, inference speed, and memory consumption. The additional matrix operations introduced negligible overhead relative to the overall computational complexity of self-attention mechanisms. The structured projections were designed to integrate seamlessly with existing model architectures, ensuring that the modifications did not introduce significant deviations from standard training procedures. Parallelization strategies were incorporated to optimize large-scale deployment, allowing the method to scale effectively across various model sizes.

To assess the effectiveness of CSMP, experimental evaluations were conducted on an open-source LLM, examining its impact on representation structure, model coherence, and downstream task performance. The experiments focused on quantifying representational compactness, semantic stability, and feature separation within token embeddings, providing empirical validation of the proposed approach.

subsections.title: IV-A Choice of Language Model
paragraphs: A recent open-source LLM was selected to serve as the experimental platform, ensuring that the evaluations were conducted on a widely accessible and reproducible architecture. The model featured a multi-layer transformer encoder with self-attention mechanisms, allowing direct integration of CSMP within its representation learning framework. The embedding dimensionality, number of attention heads, and layer depth were configured to match standard benchmarks, providing a representative setting for evaluating the method.

The model underwent standard training procedures using publicly available datasets, ensuring that the evaluations reflected real-world usage scenarios. The structured manifold projections were integrated into the intermediate transformer layers without requiring modifications to the model’s pretraining process, allowing for direct comparison with the baseline architecture.

subsections.title: IV-B Evaluation Metrics
paragraphs: To quantify the impact of CSMP, multiple evaluation metrics were employed to assess representation refinement and structural consistency. Representation compactness was measured through the effective dimensionality of token embeddings, capturing the extent to which the learned features occupied a well-defined subspace. Orthogonality constraints were evaluated through matrix condition numbers, ensuring that the projections maintained well-structured geometric properties.

Feature separability was analyzed through clustering metrics that quantified the distinction between semantic token groups within the projected embeddings. Sequence coherence was measured through perplexity scores and attention alignment metrics, ensuring that the modifications preserved the language model’s ability to generate coherent and contextually relevant text. The evaluations provided a comprehensive assessment of the impact of structured manifold projection on representation learning.

subsections.title: IV-C Experimental Procedure
paragraphs: The model was trained with and without CSMP integration to compare the effects of structured representation refinement. The experiments involved pretraining the model on standard benchmark datasets, applying the manifold projection transformations, and evaluating their impact on internal feature distributions. The structured projection matrices were initialized based on statistical properties of the learned embeddings, ensuring consistency across training iterations.

Computational resources included high-performance GPUs optimized for deep learning workloads, ensuring efficient execution of large-scale experiments. The evaluations were conducted across multiple training configurations, allowing for analysis of the method’s effectiveness under varying model sizes and dataset distributions. The experimental results provided insights into the ability of CSMP to refine LLM representations while maintaining computational efficiency and linguistic coherence.

Experimental evaluations were conducted to assess the impact of Contextual Subspace Manifold Projection (CSMP) on representation compactness, feature separability, and contextual coherence within a transformer-based large language model. Quantitative analyses compared baseline model behavior with and without structured subspace refinement, ensuring that improvements in representation structure did not compromise linguistic integrity or model coherence. The evaluation encompassed multiple statistical measures, embedding space visualizations, and performance metrics across diverse linguistic tasks, ensuring that conclusions reflected a comprehensive assessment of representational improvements.

subsections.title: V-A Representation Compactness and Orthogonality
paragraphs: The extent to which embeddings occupied a well-defined subspace was analyzed through the effective dimensionality of token representations and the orthogonality of learned feature vectors. The application of CSMP was expected to refine token embeddings by reducing anisotropy and ensuring more uniform distribution across the representation space. Table I presents the effective rank and condition number of the embedding matrix across different model layers, revealing an improvement in feature compactness when structured projections were applied.

The projection-based refinement led to a reduction in the effective rank of the embedding matrix, indicating improved feature compaction without excessive loss of representational diversity. Simultaneously, the condition number of the embedding space decreased progressively across transformer layers, implying greater numerical stability in representation utilization. Figure 1 illustrates the distribution of pairwise cosine similarities between feature vectors before and after CSMP integration, demonstrating a shift toward more uniform geometric properties.

The increased density in mid-range similarity values following CSMP application indicated a shift toward more uniform embedding distributions, reducing excessive redundancy while preserving critical semantic relationships.

subsections.title: V-B Feature Separability and Clustering Properties
paragraphs: An evaluation of token clustering within the refined representation space was conducted to determine whether CSMP contributed to improved feature separability. Embeddings were projected into a lower-dimensional space, where clustering coefficients and inter-cluster distances were measured. Table II summarizes the silhouette coefficient and Davies–Bouldin index across multiple clustering configurations.

subsections.title: V-C Impact on Perplexity and Sequence Coherence
paragraphs: The application of CSMP was further evaluated through perplexity metrics on benchmark datasets, assessing its impact on language generation coherence. The perplexity values across multiple dataset splits were compared, as shown in Table III.

The minor reduction in perplexity values indicated that structural modifications to token embeddings did not degrade language modeling performance. The refinement in representation structure improved feature expressiveness while preserving the model’s ability to generate coherent sequences.

subsections.title: V-D Stability of Representation Across Model Layers
paragraphs: An assessment was conducted to examine how CSMP influenced representation stability across successive transformer layers. Variability in token embeddings was analyzed through layer-wise Euclidean distance shifts, measuring how significantly token representations changed as they progressed through the network. Table IV presents the average embedding displacement across multiple layers.

The introduction of CSMP led to a reduction in inter-layer shifts, indicating that embeddings experienced more gradual and structured transformations as they propagated through the network. A decrease in variation suggested that CSMP enhanced feature consistency, reducing abrupt changes that could lead to representational instability.

subsections.title: V-E Token Frequency Influence on Representation Refinement
paragraphs: An evaluation was performed to assess whether token frequency influenced the extent of representational adjustments through CSMP. Low-, medium-, and high-frequency tokens were analyzed separately to determine how their embedding structures were affected. Figure 2 presents the average cosine similarity before and after CSMP application across different token frequency categories.

Higher-frequency tokens exhibited smaller modifications, suggesting that CSMP primarily influenced lower-frequency tokens, which tend to suffer from less refined representations in baseline models. The ability to improve representation structure for infrequent tokens without substantially altering common words demonstrated that CSMP selectively refined embedding distributions without introducing excessive distortions.

subsections.title: V-F Impact on Sentence-Level Semantic Similarity
paragraphs: To assess whether CSMP influenced sentence-level semantics, similarity metrics were computed across pairs of paraphrased and non-paraphrased sentences before and after applying the structured projection. The cosine similarity of sentence embeddings was measured, with results summarized in Table V.

The refinement through CSMP led to a modest increase in similarity scores for paraphrased and semantically related sentences, suggesting that representation adjustments preserved underlying linguistic structures. The negligible change for unrelated sentences indicated that the modifications did not introduce artificial proximity between semantically distinct content, maintaining the robustness of representation learning.

subsections.title: V-G Gradient Magnitude Distribution Across Training Iterations
paragraphs: To analyze how CSMP influenced model optimization dynamics, gradient magnitude distributions were examined across training iterations. The standard deviation of gradient updates across all model parameters was recorded at multiple checkpoints, capturing whether the structured refinement altered optimization behavior. Figure 3 presents the gradient magnitude distributions at selected training iterations.

The structured projection led to slightly lower gradient magnitudes across training steps, indicating that the refined representation structure reduced excessive parameter updates. The more stable gradient distributions suggested that CSMP introduced smoother optimization behavior, potentially enhancing training convergence without requiring additional regularization techniques.

The findings demonstrated that Contextual Subspace Manifold Projection (CSMP) introduced measurable improvements in representation structure within a large language model, contributing to enhanced feature compactness, improved semantic separability, and more stable token embeddings across layers. The reduction in anisotropy within the learned representations suggested that CSMP promoted a more uniform allocation of feature activations, reducing the concentration of embeddings within a constrained region of the high-dimensional space. The observed improvements in inter-cluster separability, as reflected through increased silhouette coefficients and reduced Davies–Bouldin index values, indicated that the structured refinement led to more distinct and well-defined representation groupings. The preservation of sequence coherence, evidenced through consistent perplexity scores, confirmed that the modifications did not interfere with the model’s ability to generate fluent and contextually appropriate outputs. The observed decrease in inter-layer representation shifts suggested that CSMP improved the stability of feature evolution throughout the transformer architecture, reinforcing the hypothesis that structured subspace constraints could mitigate abrupt variations in token representations. When compared against standard representation learning approaches that rely on implicit optimization objectives, the structured intervention provided a direct mechanism to refine embedding distributions while preserving computational efficiency.

While the approach demonstrated promising characteristics, several constraints were identified that could influence its practical deployment across different architectures and application domains. The dependency on predefined projection matrices introduced a reliance on carefully structured subspace definitions, potentially limiting adaptability across varying linguistic distributions or domain-specific representations. The computational overhead associated with the structured projection operations, although relatively minor, introduced additional matrix multiplications that could become significant when scaling to extremely large models or high-dimensional embedding spaces. The extent to which CSMP generalizes across varying datasets, particularly in low-resource settings where token frequency distributions differ significantly, remained an open question that warrants further investigation. The reliance on static projection matrices raised considerations regarding whether adaptive subspace constraints, informed through self-supervised learning objectives, could further enhance the flexibility of the approach. The interpretability of the refined representation structure, while indirectly inferred through clustering and similarity metrics, remained subject to additional analysis to determine how specific linguistic features were affected through the projection operations.

Several directions for expanding CSMP could be considered to enhance its applicability and effectiveness across a broader range of language model architectures and use cases. The introduction of adaptive subspace constraints, where the projection basis is learned dynamically rather than predefined, could enable the model to adjust its representational structure based on task-specific or domain-specific requirements. The integration of hierarchical manifold projections, where embeddings are refined at multiple structural levels rather than through a single projection step, could provide more granular control over representation evolution across different layers of the transformer network. The extension of CSMP to multimodal learning settings, where textual representations interact with vision or audio modalities, could further explore how structured constraints influence cross-modal feature integration. Future research could investigate whether subspace refinements provide additional advantages in low-data regimes, where feature compactness and structural efficiency become critical for optimizing generalization under limited training conditions. The broader implications of structured manifold constraints on interpretability and robustness also merit further exploration, particularly in contexts where ensuring consistency across adversarially perturbed inputs or distributional shifts is an essential requirement.

Contextual Subspace Manifold Projection introduced a structured refinement mechanism that reconfigured token embeddings through controlled subspace transformations, ensuring that representations evolved with improved compactness, feature separability, and stability across transformer layers. The structured constraints imposed through the projection operation led to a reduction in anisotropy, facilitating a more uniform utilization of the representational space while preserving linguistic coherence. The empirical evaluations demonstrated that refined token embeddings maintained semantic consistency while exhibiting more structured geometric properties, as evidenced through improvements in clustering metrics, cosine similarity distributions, and inter-layer representation shifts. The refinement process preserved perplexity values across diverse linguistic tasks, indicating that the representational modifications did not degrade sequence coherence or language generation quality. The structured intervention effectively mitigated excessive redundancy in token embeddings, reducing unnecessary overlap between feature activations while maintaining sufficient representational expressiveness. The observed improvements in stability across successive transformer layers suggested that the method contributed to more predictable and interpretable representation evolution, mitigating abrupt variations that could lead to inconsistencies in token-level feature distributions. The evaluation of gradient magnitude distributions indicated that the structured refinement introduced a smoother optimization trajectory, potentially reducing the extent of unnecessary parameter updates and stabilizing training dynamics. The ability to selectively refine internal activations without requiring modifications to pretraining objectives or additional gradient-based optimizations demonstrated the flexibility of the approach, highlighting its potential utility in enhancing LLM representation learning without requiring extensive computational overhead. The structured projection operation provided a direct mechanism for imposing geometric constraints on token embeddings, ensuring that representations adhered to well-defined subspaces while maintaining the adaptability required for general-purpose language modeling. The empirical results suggested that enforcing structured manifold constraints on token embeddings contributed to more stable, compact, and interpretable representations, reinforcing the importance of geometric considerations in neural representation learning.
